title: Semantics-Based Verification of an Implemented Shor Oracle for ECDLP in Qrisp
abstract: Shor-style quantum algorithms for the elliptic-curve discrete logarithm problem (ECDLP) are highly sensitive to the exact semantics of their group-operation oracles. Consequently, minor implementation choices can invalidate the intended mathematical model and lead to misleading conclusions. This paper introduces a semantics-first verification perspective for an end-to-end, compilable ECDLP implementation built on Qrisp. We specify the implemented oracle at the level of program semantics, derive refinement-style verification obligations for its key components, and provide a high-level complexity argument for the resulting oracle family. A small case study highlights that (i) the core point-update primitive agrees with a classical reference on well-formed inputs, yet (ii) controlled execution may violate the expected control law under the evaluated toolchain, despite a passing trivial control sanity check. These results position semantic auditing as a practical prerequisite for trustworthy ECDLP-oriented quantum software.
summary: This paper proposes a method for verifying that quantum software implementations of ECDLP oracles correctly match their mathematical specifications. It demonstrates that even when individual components work correctly, the compilation process in frameworks like Qrisp can introduce errors in controlled operations. This highlights the need for semantic auditing in the development of trustworthy quantum algorithms.
main_problem: The discrepancy between abstract mathematical oracles for the Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP) and their actual implementation in quantum programming frameworks like Qrisp, which can lead to invalid algorithmic conclusions.
main_result: The paper establishes a semantics-first verification framework and identifies a specific case where a compiled controlled-gate implementation fails to satisfy the expected control law, despite passing standalone tests.
method: The authors use a refinement-style verification approach, defining correctness obligations across multiple layers of implementation and using a well-formedness predicate to handle exceptional inputs.
model_or_system: The study uses the Qrisp quantum programming framework to implement a Shor-style oracle for ECDLP over a prime field Fp, specifically utilizing Short Weierstrass curves.
key_observables: Circuit complexity metrics including qubit count, circuit depth, T-count, and CX-count, as well as the verification of the control law and point-update accuracy.
important_parameters: The field size parameter m = ceil(log2 p) and the specific small-scale instance parameters p=5, a=3, b=3.
main_assumptions: The complexity analysis assumes that underlying modular arithmetic and inversion primitives compile to uniform polynomial-size reversible circuits.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the composition of the oracle, showing how the accumulator register is updated via sequential bitwise-controlled updates.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the semantic gap problem, proposes a refinement-based verification framework, provides a complexity argument for the oracle family, and concludes with a case study demonstrating a control-law violation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Oracle composition in the implementation. The accumulator R is updated by two scalar-multiplication-and-add routines, controlled bitwise by x1 and x2, yielding R ⊕x1G ⊕x2P. Here ⊕denotes the elliptic-curve group operation under the imple- mentation’s point encoding, not bitwise XOR.
